description: Housing resources and strategies for people with felony convictions

housingTypes.description: Structured living for 6-24 months after release. Often includes job training, counseling, and case management.
howToFind: Contact your parole/probation officer — they have a list of approved transitional housing
Call 211 and ask for 'reentry housing' or 'transitional housing'
Search the Federal Bureau of Prisons Residential Reentry Center list: bop.gov/locations/rrc/
Contact your state's Department of Corrections reentry division
housingTypes.cost: Free to $400/month (income-based, often covered by reentry programs)
housingTypes.restrictions: Sex offenders may have limited options due to proximity rules. Some facilities are substance-abuse specific.
housingTypes.tips: Apply BEFORE release if possible. Waitlists can be 2-8 weeks.

housingTypes.description: Self-supporting, democratically-run sober living homes. No paid staff. Residents manage the house together.
howToFind: oxfordhouse.org — search by state
Call Oxford House World Services: 301-587-2916
Over 3,000 houses in 44 states
housingTypes.restrictions: Must be in recovery (substance abuse). Must stay sober. Must pay rent. Must be employed or actively seeking.
housingTypes.tips: One of the best options for returning citizens in recovery. Democratic vote to admit new members. Very supportive community.

housingTypes.description: Free 6-month residential program including housing, meals, and work therapy.
howToFind: Walk into any Salvation Army — ask about ARC program
salvationarmyusa.org/usn/provide-shelter/
housingTypes.cost: FREE — work therapy program covers costs
housingTypes.restrictions: Must participate in work therapy (thrift store). Faith-based environment. Substance abuse focus.
housingTypes.tips: No ID required to enter. No background check. Walk in same day. Meals and housing provided immediately.

housingTypes.description: Emergency and transitional shelter through faith-based organizations. Usually free.
howToFind: agrm.org — Association of Gospel Rescue Missions (300+ missions)
Call 211 for local rescue mission
Search '[your city] rescue mission'
housingTypes.restrictions: Faith-based programming (chapel services). Some require sobriety.
housingTypes.tips: Best for immediate shelter when you have nothing. Many provide meals, clothing, job training. No questions asked.

housingTypes.description: Individual landlords and small property management companies are more likely to rent to people with records than large corporate complexes.
howToFind: Search Craigslist, Facebook Marketplace for 'room for rent' or 'private landlord'
Drive through neighborhoods looking for 'For Rent' signs (small landlords)
Ask at local churches — members sometimes rent rooms
Contact local reentry organizations — they often have landlord lists
helpexit.org and rentingwithfelonies.com for felon-friendly landlord databases
housingTypes.convictionFriendly: Varies — individual landlords are generally more flexible than corporate
housingTypes.restrictions: Sex offenders face proximity restrictions. Landlords can legally deny based on conviction in most states.
tips: Small/private landlords are 3x more likely to rent to someone with a record than large property management companies
Be upfront about your record — landlords appreciate honesty
Get a reference letter from your parole officer, case manager, or employer
Offer to pay extra security deposit
If denied, ask: 'Is there anything I can provide to change your decision?' (reference letters, proof of employment, etc.)

housingTypes.description: Federal program that pays 70% of rent. Administered by local housing authorities.
howToFind: Contact your local Public Housing Authority (PHA)
Search: hud.gov/program_offices/public_indian_housing/pha/contacts
Apply when the waitlist opens (check monthly)
housingTypes.cost: You pay 30% of your income. Program pays the rest.
housingTypes.convictionFriendly: Partial — most felons are eligible EXCEPT those with lifetime sex offender registration or methamphetamine production conviction in federally-assisted housing.
housingTypes.restrictions: Long waitlists (6 months to 5+ years depending on area). Must meet income requirements (<50% Area Median Income). Background check.
tips: Apply to MULTIPLE housing authorities — each has its own waitlist
Some PHAs have preferences for veterans, homeless, or disabled — check if you qualify
While waiting, ask about 'Project-Based Vouchers' which may have shorter waitlists
HUD does NOT have a blanket ban on renting to felons. In 2016, HUD issued guidance that blanket bans may violate Fair Housing Act.

housingTypes.description: Structured homes for people in recovery. Drug/alcohol free environment with peer support.
howToFind: soberhousing.net — national directory
afterinpatient.com — verified listings
Ask your substance abuse counselor or parole officer
housingTypes.restrictions: Must maintain sobriety. Regular drug testing. Must follow house rules.
housingTypes.tips: Many will work with you on payment if you're newly released. Some accept state reentry funding.

housingTypes.description: Short-term rental assistance (3-24 months) to help people quickly exit homelessness.
howToFind: Contact your local Continuum of Care (CoC) — hud.gov/program_offices/comm_planning/coc
Call 211 and ask for 'rapid re-housing'
Apply through your local homeless services agency
housingTypes.cost: Subsidized — you pay what you can afford, program covers the rest
housingTypes.convictionFriendly: Yes — HUD rapid re-housing has no criminal history exclusion
housingTypes.restrictions: Must meet HUD definition of homeless (shelter, street, institutional)
housingTypes.tips: If you're in a shelter or on the street, you likely qualify. These programs also help with move-in costs (deposit, first/last month).

resources.name: Atlanta Housing Authority
resources.notes: Section 8 waitlist — check monthly for openings

resources.name: Atlanta Mission Transitional Housing
resources.notes: Free transitional housing with job training

resources.name: Decatur Cooperative Ministry
resources.notes: Emergency housing + rapid re-housing in DeKalb County

resources.name: LA Homeless Services Authority (LAHSA)
resources.notes: Coordinated entry for LA County housing

resources.name: Homeboy Industries Housing
resources.notes: Housing assistance as part of reentry services

resources.name: Amity Foundation
resources.notes: Therapeutic communities for returning citizens in LA

resources.name: Dismas House Nashville
resources.notes: Transitional housing specifically for returning citizens

resources.name: MDHA (Metro Nashville Housing Authority)
resources.notes: Section 8 and public housing — check waitlist status
